To calculate the probability that the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in Version B is less than the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in Version A, we consider the difference in sample means. Let $ \bar{X}_A $ and $ \bar{X}_B $ denote the sample means of interaction times for Version A and Version B, respectively. The sampling distribution of the difference $ \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B $ is approximately normal due to the Central Limit Theorem, with mean $ \mu_{\bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B} = \mu_A - \mu_B = 45 - 40 = 5 $ seconds and standard deviation $ \sigma_{\bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_A^2}{n} + \frac{\sigma_B^2}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{25}{30} + \frac{9}{30}} = \sqrt{\frac{34}{30}} \approx 1.06 $ seconds. We want $ P(\bar{X}_B < \bar{X}_A) = P(\bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B > 0) $. Standardizing, we get $ Z = \frac{0 - 5}{1.06} \approx -4.717 $. The probability corresponding to this Z-score is essentially 1, meaning that the average interaction time for Version B is almost certainly less than that for Version A.
